coredata.dc:title: Label-free fluorescence aptasensor for sensitive determination of bisphenol S by the salt-adjusted FRET between CQDs and MoS2
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Kai
Zhang, Wentao
Zhang, Yuhuan
Huang, Lunjie
Wang, Rong
Yue, Xiaoyue
Zhu, Wenxin
Zhang, Daohong
Zhang, Xu
Zhang, Ying
Wang, Jianlong
coredata.dc:description: Although the salt-adjusted properties of nanomaterials have been extensively used for colorimetric assays of all kinds of analytes, their applications in the design of fluorescence sensor are very limited. Herein, a novel fluorescence aptasensor was developed for the sensitive determination of Bisphenol S (BPS) by the salt-adjusted fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between the zero-dimensional emitter (CQDs) and the two-dimensional absorber (MoS2 nanosheets) and the roles of specific discrimination and protective effect of anti-BPS aptamer to layered MoS2. It is shown that layered MoS2 can serve as a good fluorescent quencher towards carbon dots with decent quenching efficiency. Importantly, the established fluorescent method achieved satisfactory results for the determination of BPS with a good linear correlation from 0.05 μM to 2 μM and a detection limit of 2 nM. It is expected that this method may offer a new direction for the application of the phenomenon of salt-assisted aggregation of nanomaterials in designing high-performance fluorescent biosensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MoS2
CQDs
Aggregation
FRET
Aptamer
BPS

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of BPS in real samples
Principle of biosensing BPS using layered MoS2 and CQDs
Characterization of layered MoS2 and CQDs
FRET quenching of CQDs induced by MoS2
The recovery effect induced by aggregation system
Detections of BPS in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was financed by Grants from National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21675127 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The scheme illustration of label-free sensor.

(A, B) SEM images of Layered MoS2 under different magnifications with the inset representing SEM measured size distribution of MoS2 nanosheets, over 100 sheets were counted. (C) AFM image of MoS2nanosheets. (D) Height profile across the MoS2 nanosheets shown in (C).
TEM images of (A) MoS2 nanosheets and (B) CQDs.

(A) Optical absorption spectrum of layered MoS2 aqueous solution. (B) Carbon dots fluorescence quenching by different contents of layered MoS2. (C) Optical absorption spectra of layered MoS2 dispersion before and after adding various concentrations of NaCl. (D) Carbon dots fluorescence quenching by MoS2 with various concentrations of NaCl. (E) Optical absorption spectra of layered MoS2 separately in the absence and presence of different concentrations of aptamer with the addition of 10 × 10−3 M NaCl. The inset shows the relationship between aptamer concentration and fluorescence intensity. (F) Fluorescence emission spectra of sensor under different conditions.

(A) Fluorescence spectrum of developed system at different concentrations of BPS. (B) Peak fluorescence change (F–F0) as a function of BPS concentration. Inset: The change of fluorescence peak is linear versus BPA concentrations during the range from 0.05 to 2 μM. (C) Specificity of the biosensor for BPS (2 μM). The peak fluorescence for other analogues was measured at the same concentration mixing with 1 μM (half) BPS and 1 μM (half) BPA.
Detection results of BPS in spiked milk.

head.ce:title: Label-free fluorescence aptasensor for sensitive determination of bisphenol S by the salt-adjusted FRET between CQDs and MoS2

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest A&F University, Yangling 712100, Shaanxi, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Although the salt-adjusted properties of nanomaterials have been extensively used for colorimetric assays of all kinds of analytes, their applications in the design of fluorescence sensor are very limited. Herein, a novel fluorescence aptasensor was developed for the sensitive determination of Bisphenol S (BPS) by the salt-adjusted fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between the zero-dimensional emitter (CQDs) and the two-dimensional absorber (MoS2 nanosheets) and the roles of specific discrimination and protective effect of anti-BPS aptamer to layered MoS2. It is shown that layered MoS2 can serve as a good fluorescent quencher towards carbon dots with decent quenching efficiency. Importantly, the established fluorescent method achieved satisfactory results for the determination of BPS with a good linear correlation from 0.05 μM to 2 μM and a detection limit of 2 nM. It is expected that this method may offer a new direction for the application of the phenomenon of salt-assisted aggregation of nanomaterials in designing high-performance fluorescent biosensors.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) attract improving attention as potential substrate for biosensing compared to conventional semiconductor quantum dots due to their attractive advantages including good aqueous solubility, stable photoluminescence, low toxicity, resistance to photobleaching, excellent biocompatibility and tunable fluorescence emission [1–3]. Owing to their inherent advantages, CQDs gain significant interest as fluorescent probes in FRET-based fluorescent biosensors [4]. Particularly, when the absorption band of the quencher overlaps with the fluorescence emission band of the donor, the fluorescence quenching occurs effectively via FRET [5,6]. Such FRET-based sensing platforms usually contain a fluorophore-labeled probe and a quencher to form a fluorescence resonance energy transfer pair, in which the distance-dependent fluorescence quenching is closely coupled with the biomolecular recognition event [7]. However, we noticed that the wide applications of florescence probes sensors were limited due to the drawback that the fluorophore-labeled processes made approaches complex, costly, and time-consuming [8–10]. Therefore, a homogeneous CQDs system independent on fluorescence labeling is encouraged with much more attention.

Since the discovery of color changes by salt-induced gold nanoparticle conjugates in 1996 [11], many types of colloidal particles have been utilized to design sensors based on measurements of aggregation-induced plasmon resonance properties changes [12,13]. Although these systems are simple and convenient, they almost focus on colorimetric assays and zero-dimensional materials, which have suffocated the wide applications of aggregation-adjusted methods. As the prominent example of emerging 2D materials, molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) has received a fair share of attention as a new platform to construct biosensor [14,15]. More importantly, with its large intrinsic bandgap and direct dispersion in aqueous solution, layered MoS2 shows prior promise for the relevant homogeneous assays [16,17]. Since Zhu and co-workers [18] firstly discovered the absorption and quenching of dye-labeled ssDNA probe by layered MoS2, considerable efforts have been devoted to the design of novel fluorescence sensors by coupling MoS2 with biomolecular recognition events [19–22]. More recently, Li and co-workers [23] constructed a label-free detection of target ssDNA based on the salt-induced aggregation and the intrinsic optical absorption of MoS2, which was more advantageous, simpler, and cheaper than the fluorescein-labeled probe ssDNA. Nevertheless, the salt-adjusted quenching property of MoS2 is still worth being explored and can serve as a novel analysis platform.

The efficient energy transfer fluorescence quenching between the zero-dimensional emitter and the two-dimensional absorber attracts increasing interest in optical detection. In particular, the transfer rate from the quantum dots to the van der Waals material decreases significantly with the thickness increase of nanomaterial, as found in MoS2 [24,25]. Taking advantage of this property, it’s possible to adjust the quenching ability of MoS2 by salt-induced aggregation and aptamer-providing protection. Thereupon, by means of the especial FRET between CQDs and MoS2, we demonstrated that the quenching effect between MoS2 and CQDs could be regulated by salt and it was feasible to render this property as an indicator to develop fluorescence sensor. Consequently, making use of the aptamer spatial conformation induced reversible aggregation switch, we successfully designed a label-free fluorescence sensor to detect BPS through metabolic FRET between CQDs and MoS2.

Molybdenum sulfide (MoS2 crystalline powder, <2 μm, 99%), 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium hexafluorophosphate (BMIPF6) (≥97.0%), BPS, bisphenol AF (BPAF), bisphenol A (BPA), 4,4-biphenol (BP) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). Sodium cholate (98%) and 2-amino-2-(hydroxymethyl)-1,3-propanediol (tris) were obtained from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Citric acids (CA), ethylenediamine (EDA), trichloroacetic acid, NaOH, and NaCl were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). DNA oligonucleotides were purchased from Sangon, Inc. (Shanghai, China), and used as received: BPS aptamer (T1), 5′-CCG GTG GGT GGT CAG GTG GGA TAG CGT TCC GCG TAT GGC CCA GCG CAT CAC GGG TTC GCA CCA-3′; FAM-labeled BPS aptamer (FAM-T1). Other chemicals were analytical reagent grade and used without further purification. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) was used throughout the experiment.

All fluorescence (FL) spectra were performed at room temperature on a PE LS-55 fluorescence spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA). The UV–vis spectra were recorded with a UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) at room temperature. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image was taken by an S-4800 (Hitachi, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were performed with an H-600 (Hitachi, Japan). AFM measurements were performed on a Bruker NanoScope V instrument in tapping mode.

Bulk MoS2 must be exfoliated into single or few layered films before engaged into aggregation and FRET system. In this report, liquid-phase exfoliation of MoS2 is carried out following previous report [26,27]. Generally, natural MoS2 powder with a bulk particle size <2 μm was exfoliated using an ionic liquid assisted grinding method. Typically, using an agate mortar with a pestle, 300 milligrams of bulk MoS2 was ground with 0.4 mL of BMIPF6 for 2 h. The grinding mixture was collected from the mortar and pestle, and then washed with acetone and 0.5% acetic acid to remove the ionic liquid. This washing cycle was repeated three times. Finally, the sediment was dispersed in 50 mL water and centrifuged at a speed of 3000 rpm for 10 min to remove the large/thick MoS2. The obtained MoS2 nanosheet dispersion was stored in 4 °C for the following investigations.

CQDs were synthesized by a facile route of microwave assisted pyrolysis method [1]. Briefly, 0.2 g of citric acid was dissolved in 5 mL of water, and 1 mL of ethylenediamine was added dropwise under vigorous magnetic stirring. After that, the mixture was pyrolyzed in a microwave oven at medium heat for 2 min and at high heat for another 2 min to form a red-brown product. Anhydrous ethanol was added and the sample was sonicated for 30 min to form muddy dispersion. Attributed to the poorer solubility of CQDs in ethanol than that of CA and EDA, the purified CQDs can be separated after several cycles of centrifugation and wash. Finally, the purified CQDs were dried at 60 °C, dissolved with a small amount of water sample, and then freeze-dried overnight at −50 °C to obtain dried powder.

Layered MoS2 dispersions (100 μL, 0.5 mg/mL) were added to different concentrations of salt (NaCl) solutions (800 μL), respectively. The as-prepared dispersions were handled with 1 min vortex at room temperature and CQDs solution (100 μL) was added to the above solution with a final concentration of 4 ng/mL. After the mixture incubates for few minutes at room temperature, the fluorescence emission spectra of the mixture was recorded.

Prior to the addition of the layered MoS2 solution, the probe aptamer with different amounts of BPS were incubated at 37 °C in buffer (600 μL, 1 mM PBS buffer solution, pH 7.4), and the mixtures were cooled to room temperature. Then, 200 μL of as-obtained layered MoS2 dispersion (0.2 mg/mL) was added to the mixed solution and incubated for 5 min. After that, a 1 min vortex was conducted at room temperature, allowing the aggregation of layered MoS2. Finally, 100 μL CQDs (40 ng/mL) was added to the solution, and the quantitive detection of BPS was achieved according to the fluorescence of CQDs. Selective detection was also performed based on the above procedure in the presence of other similar bisphenolic compounds (BPA, BP, BPAF).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of BPS in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In real sample determinations, milk samples purchased from local super-markets were chosen. For the fluorescent determination, milk samples should be treated to remove protein. Briefly, milk samples were mixed with 2 M trichloroacetic acid solution. After thorough sonication for 15 min, the mixtures were centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 15 min. Subsequently, the supernatant was collected and the pH value was adjusted to neutrality with NaOH. Then the solution was filtered using a 0.22 m membrane. Finally, the filtrate was collected into 3 tubes and supplemented with BPS solutions for the final concentration of 0.5 μM, 5 μM, and 20 μM. The prepared spiked samples were further subjected to the fluorescent detection [28,29].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of biosensing BPS using layered MoS2 and CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The principle of the fluorescent sensor is shown in Scheme 1 . In the absence of target molecule (BPS), the unfolded and flexible aptamer is adsorbed onto the surface of MoS2 nanosheet to form stable aptamer/MoS2 nanosheet architecture, bringing the protection for inhibiting aggregation of MoS2 in salt solution. Consequently, the fluorescence of CQDs is quenched via energy transfer between CQDs and MoS2. On the contrary, in the presence of BPS, the conformation of the aptamer specific for BPS is switched from a random coil to aptamer/BPS complex. Such a rigid conformation will mitigate van der Waals force between aptamer and MoS2 nanosheet, keeping the aptamer probes away from the nanosheet surface, which induces the aggregation of MoS2 and consequent fluorescence restoration. Therefore, to measure the quenching activity of MoS2, the fluorescent intensity of CQDs is recorded correspondingly, which provides a quantitative readout of the target molecule.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of layered MoS2 and CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
After grinding and then centrifugation to remove excessive surfactant, the layered MoS2 nanosheets suspension was obtained to be use. The optical absorption spectrum of our MoS2 suspension was recorded using a UV–vis spectrometer (Fig. 3A
). The direct excitonic transition from the deep valence to the conduction band results in absorption bands at 402 and 465 nm; the absorption peaks at 617 and 677 nm can be attributed to the excitonic transitions from the K point of the Brillouin zone, indicating an increase in the bandgap of the nanoscale MoS2 [30]. The typical morphologies and structures of the MoS2 nanosheets were observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Fig. 1A, and B) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Fig. 2A), primarily showing the nanoscale of as-exfoliated MoS2 with majority plane size of 90–120 nm. Furthermore, the thickness of the lamellar structure of individual MoS2 nanosheets was measured by atomic force microscopy (AFM) and the results are shown in Fig. 1C, and D. These images clearly show a uniform view with a typical thickness of around 1.4 nm, corresponding to about double monolayers of MoS2 [31].

The morphology of the CQDs was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). It is can be seen that the CQDs have a uniform dispersion without any apparent aggregation with a narrow size ranging from 2 to 5 nm (Fig. 2B, Fig. S2A, and B), which means favourable applicability in fluorescence sensor [5,32]. Furthermore, we investigated the optical properties of the as-obtained CQDs. Fig. S2E, it was seen that the maximum fluorescent excitation wavelength and emission wavelength of the CQDs were 360 and 455 nm, respectively. Additionally, the absorption spectrum shows that the CQDs exhibit a significant absorption band centered at approximately 360 nm (Fig. S2E), which corresponds to the optimum fluorescence excitation peak and should be attributed to the n-π* transition of CO or COH on the surface of the CQDs [33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: FRET quenching of CQDs induced by MoS2
ce:section.ce:para: It has been known for many years that most of the transition-metal dichalcogenide possess intrinsic fluorescence quenching properties [34]. As a decent nanoquencher, MoS2 could efficiently quench fluorescence due to the electronic energy transfer (EET) and resonance energy transfer (RET) [18]. What’s more, CQDs are chosen owing to its unique properties, such as stable photoluminescence, high fluorescent activity, and good compatibility, which has emerged as a promising material over the past few decades in the area of numerous fluorescent analysis [35–37]. The fluorescence quenching ability of layered MoS2 toward CQDs was evaluated upon mixing the fluorescent CQDs and the prepared MoS2 nanosheets. By adding equal volume of MoS2 solutions of different concentration, the fluorescent intensity of CQDs experienced a decreasing process as shown in Fig. 3B. During this process, when CQDs are located in near surface of MoS2 nanosheets, the transport of an excited electron-hole pair from emitter (CQDs) to acceptor (MoS2) may occur. Such FRET provides a mechanism for nonradiative deactivation of the excited state of CQDs, leading to a decrease in emission intensity. In the absence of MoS2, the fluorescence spectrum of CQDs exhibits strong fluorescence emission. Moreover, the ultimate quenching efficiency of layered MoS2 nanosheets reached 97% when the final MoS2 concentration was 48 μg/mL. The above results indicated that layered MoS2 nanosheets could serve as a good fluorescent quencher towards carbon dots with excellent efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The recovery effect induced by aggregation system
ce:section.ce:para: Considerable research efforts have been devoted to salt-induced aggregation behaviors of nanomaterials with various interesting phenomena in a range of applications. In our experiment, the quenching ability of layered MoS2 was studied in the presence of high concentration of Na+ ions through its reaction with CQDs. According to the charge screening theory, layered MoS2 will tardily aggregate in the presence of salt, which will induce the change of absorption spectra. To fully improve the efficiency of analysis, principally limited by the lengthy time-dependent aggregation which may result in inaccurate determination, a 1 min vortex was conducted instead of 8 h waiting for aggregation and sedimentation. From Fig. 3C, it is observed that with the addition of 100 × 10−3 M NaCl, the absorbance of layered MoS2 dispersion peaked at 402 nm (A exciton), 465 nm (B exciton), 617 nm (C exciton), and 677 nm (D exciton) decreased, which suggests that the salt-induced aggregation resulted in the decrease of single or few-layered MoS2 in aqueous solution [23]. The SEM image of MoS2 after salt disposing further suggests the aggregation of MoS2 nanosheets (Fig. S3). With the certitude of aggregation of layered MoS2, further investigation toward corresponding quenching capacity of MoS2 was explored. From Fig. 3D, as expected, the fluorescence intensity is enhanced with the increase of the salt concentration, which arises from the aggregation of layered MoS2, suggesting the salt-adjusted quenching ability of MoS2. Meanwhile, an increasing amount of added salt can provide additional shielding on the charge surface to reduce the electric repulsive forces between nanosheets then promotes their aggregation and weakens fluorescence quenching. In the aggregation process, with the decrease of single or few-layered MoS2, the insufficient accessible reactive surface area make the CQDs too crowded to contact with MoS2, triggering the subdued quenching of carbon dots. Moreover, the FRET rate between the quantum dot and MoS2 nanosheets increases significantly with the decrease of MoS2 thickness, which arises from reduced dielectric screening in thin layer semiconductors having usually large permittivity and a strong in-plane transition dipole moment [25]. Both of these factors contribute to the salt-controlled adjustable quenching ability of MoS2.

Furthermore, as soon as a certain amount of label-free aptamer was added to the layered MoS2 to form layered aptamer-MoS2 complex, the salt-induced aggregation phenomenon of original layered MoS2 was inhibited, regularly, which could be ascribed to that the negatively charged DNA backbone on the surface of layered MoS2 increased individual nanosheets electrostatic repulsion and thus resisted salt-induced aggregation. As shown in Fig. 3E, in the presence of aptamer, the coagulation of layered MoS2 was inhibited, corresponding to the increased concentration of aptamer. When the concentration of aptamer increased to 15 nM, the coagulation of layered MoS2 was likely to be highly inhibited and thus the fluorescence quenching ability of MoS2 was maintained. The affinity experiment of as-obtained layered MoS2 to aptamer was designed, as shown in Fig. S3A. Upon the addition of BPS, the rigid construction of aptamer T1 forms with the binding of BPS and the solution shows significant fluorescence restoration (curve b), suggesting that the interaction between folded aptamer and MoS2 is weaker than that between random coil aptamer and MoS2. Combined with the protection effect of aptamer, the distinguishable affinity of MoS2 toward differential structural states of aptamer could offer a new approach to detect a broad range of analytes. When aptamer forms the rigid conformation with the binding of target, it cannot stably adsorb on layered MoS2 because the ring structure of nucleobases is effectively shielded within the rigidly stereo conformation of aptamer, and thus the addition of folded aptamer cannot inhibit the coagulation of layered MoS2 in salt solution. In our designed experiment, label-free aptamer can bind with BPS to form aptamer/BPS composite (Fig. 3F). Compared to the layered MoS2 protected by aptamer only (curve c, Fig. 3F), the increase of fluorescence was observed as a result of attenuate protection of layered MoS2 with the formation of aptamer/BPS composite (curve b, Fig. 3F), which resulted from the weaker inhibition effect due to the coagulation of layered MoS2 under optimized concentration of Na+ ion.

As the initial alternative for the replacement of bisphenol A (BPA), bisphenol S (BPS) have been recently demonstrated to be toxic [38–40]. However, on account of the inexistence of aptamer, the trace determination of BPS still stays in the cumbersome traditional technologies [41–43]. In our experiment we use this adjustable FRET aptasensor platform for BPS detection. Fig. 4A shows the fluorescence emission spectra in the presence of different concentrations of BPS. As expected, the fluorescence intensity was intensified by the increase of the BPS concentration from 0.05 μM to 50 μM. As shown in Fig. 4B, F–F0 was in a linear relationship along with concentrations of BPS from 0.05 μM to 2 μM and the linear regression equation is y (F–F0) = 20.984x + 2.820 with a correlation coefficient of 0.991, where F0 is the fluorescence intensity of CQDs in the absence of analyte and F is the intensified fluorescence intensity after the addition of BPS. The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 0.002 μM, as calculated according to the 3 S0/S criterion (based on 3 times the signal-to-noise ratio), where S is the slope of the calibration curve and S0 represents the standard deviation of a blank (n = 6). Moreover, the calibration curve with the linear range from 2 to 30 μM is gained and the linear equation is y (F − F0) = 2.967x + 38.386 with a correlation coefficient of 0.991. Although the LOD of BPS of this method cannot mention in the same breath with that of the chromatographic analysis, it is much lower than that of previously reported obtained by rapid methods [44,45] and gets access to conventional instrumental methods, this mean remains attractive and meaningful for the groundbreaking research on realizing rapid and on-site detection of BPS.

Layered MoS2 is one of the typical 2D materials whose loose stacked layers are comprised of covalently bonded Mo atoms and S atoms, in the stoichiometry MoS2. Previous researchers have found that MoS2 nanosheets are able to quench light of fluorescent dye [18,19]. And this experiment reveals that MoS2 can also quench the fluorescent from other nanomaterials like carbon dots and form a highly efficient FRET pair at the same time. Compared to aptasensor made of fluorescent dye, carbon dots provide strong and stable fluorescence which can improve the sensitivity and efficiency [35]. This novel fluorescence aptasensor, based on the aggregation-adjusted quenching property of layered MoS2 nanosheets, can not only give out strong fluorescent signal in the absence of BPS but also change sensitively with target addition thus providing possible quantitive sensing for BPS analysis.

Target specificity of the method should be confirmed so that potential interferences from other substances can be eliminated. Fluorescence response of CQDs was inspected in the presence of other similar electroactive bisphenolic compounds such as BPA, BP, BPAF. As shown in Fig. 4C, the introduction of BP and BPAF to system presents negligible fluorescent recovery of CQDs, while BPA has a certain degree of inevitable signal, which is caused by the intrinsic recognition ability of aptamer. Nevertheless, taking advantage of the prominently different solubility in methylbenzene, a pretreatment step by using methylbenzene as the extractant could separate BPS or BPA from the mix solution, bring the multi-analysis of BPA and BPS. The fabricated fluorescence method is low-cost, fast, and highly sensitive and this aggregation-based assay format can be finished within a few minutes. Nevertheless, compared with other methods based on functionalized nanomaterials, such as graphene, Au nanoparticles, and quantum dots, the developed sensor was simple and reliable, because layered MoS2 was used without functionalization and responses were directly provided by its intrinsic property of fluorescence quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detections of BPS in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to demonstrate the practical application of this proposed method, standard recovery experiments were accordingly carried out using milk samples. The milk was pretreated by centrifugation with a certain amount of trichloroacetic acid. Then, three experimental groups were designed by adding various amounts of BPS (0.5, 5 and 20 μM) into the pretreated milk to test the recovery. All of the milk samples were detected by our fluorescent method. As shown in Table 1 , the recoveries of the proposed method are in the range of 92.0%-104.2%, indicating that the proposed sensing platform is accurate and can be used for practical detection of BPS in relevant real samples [29].

In this contribution, a novel fluorescence aptasensor was developed based on the tunable FRET between CQDs and MoS2 for sensitive determination of BPS. It is shown that layered MoS2 nanosheets can directly quench CODs in an efficient and quick way. By converting the conformational state of the aptamer, it provides an adjustable protection to inhibit the salt-accelerated coagulation of MoS2, which renders it as promising platforms for switchable sensor. The established fluorescent method achieved satisfying results for the determination of BPS with a good linear correlation from 0.05 μM to 2 μM and a detection limit of 2 nM. It demonstrated that the exploitation of tunable quenching capacity of MoS2 greatly enhanced the sensitivity for the BPS detection with a quick and cheap pathway. Importantly, by changing the kind of aptamers, the present detection method will be fairly easy to extend to the detections of other biomolecules. At the same time, it is expected that this method may offer a new direction for the application of salt-assisted nanomaterials aggregation in designing high-performance fluorescent biosensors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financed by Grants from National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.21675127).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.12.116.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Kai Chen obtained his B.S. degree in 2015 from Northwest A&F University. Now he is a graduate student in College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest A&F University. His research mainly focuses on the green systhesis of nanomaterials for the construction of facile sensors.

Wentao Zhang obtained his B.S. degree in 2013 and M.S. degree in 2015 from Northwest A&F University. Now he is a graduate student in College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest A&F University. His current research mainly focuses on rational design of nanomaterials for biological applications.

Yuhuan Zhang received her B.S. degree in 2013 from Yan Tai University and M.S. degree in 2016 from Northwest A&F University. Now she is a graduate student in College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest A&F University. Her current research mainly focuses on the design of sensors for cancer cell.

Lunjie Huang obtained his B.S. degree in 2015 from Wuhan Polytechnic University. Now he is a graduate student in College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest A&F University. His research mainly focuses on the design of sensors for colorimetric detection.

Rong Wang received her B.S. degree in 2013 from Hunan Agricultural University and M.S. degree in 2016 from Northwest A&F University. Now she is a graduate student in College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest A&F University. Her current research mainly focuses on solar photocatalytic disinfection and its mechanism study.

Xiaoyue Yue received her B.S. degree in 2011 Henan University of Science and Technology, M.S. degree in 2014 from Northwest A&F University and Ph.D. degree in 2017 from Northwest A&F University Now she he is a teacher in College of Food Science and Engineering, Zhengzhou University of Light Industry. Her current research mainly focuses on the design of sensors for food detections.

Wenxin Zhu received his B.S. degree in 2014 and M.S. degree in 2016 from Northwest A&F University. Now he is a graduate student in College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest A&F University. His current research mainly focuses on rational surface/interface design and synthesis of functional nanomaterials for electrocatalysis applications.

Daohong Zhang received her Ph.D. degree in Chinese Academy of Agricultural Sciences in 2011. Currently, she is an Associate Professor in College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest A&F University. Her main research interests are the Rapid detection of poisonous and harmful substances with immunology methods.

Xu Zhang obtained her B.S. degree in 2016 from Northwest A&F University. Now he is a graduate student in College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest A&F University. Her research mainly focuses on the design of functional nanomaterials for combating bacterial resistance.

Ying Zhang obtained her B.S. degree in 2016 from Shandong University of Technology. Now he is a graduate student in College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest A&F University. Her research mainly focuses on the design of sensors for colorimetric detection.

Jianlong Wang received his Ph.D. degree in Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry (CIAC), Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2008. During 2008–2011, he carried out postdoctoral research at Worcester Polytechnic Institute. Currently, he is a full Professor in College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest A&F University. His research mainly focuses on rational design and synthesis of functional nanomaterials for sensing and catalysis applications.
